responses.text: The provided article discusses the April 2021 announcement of the European Super League, a highly controversial breakaway competition proposed by twelve leading soccer clubs.
As noted in the article, the founding members included six prominent clubs from the English Premier League, three from Spain's La Liga, and three from Italy's Serie A.
Top German and French clubs, most notably Bayern Munich and Paris Saint-Germain, rejected the proposal and refused to join.
The Super League model intended to grant permanent, non-relegatable status to 15 founding clubs, starkly contrasting with the merit-based, open qualification of the UEFA Champions League.
Financed by the American investment bank JPMorgan Chase, the breakaway league promised massive initial payouts to its founding members.
However, the target article was published as an unprecedented global backlash arose from fans, domestic leagues, UEFA, FIFA, and government officials.
Intense grassroots protests, particularly from supporters demonstrating outside stadiums like Chelsea's Stamford Bridge, quickly undermined the political and social viability of the project.
Within 48 hours of the announcement, all six English clubs capitulated to the pressure and formally withdrew from the Super League.
Shortly thereafter, most other founding clubs suspended their involvement, ensuring the Super League as originally proposed could not proceed.

While subsequent legal battles led to a 2023 European Court of Justice ruling that UEFA had acted unlawfully in completely blocking the project, the original breakaway format detailed in this article remains abandoned.
